> Concepts: [[commitment-continuum]] · [[control-the-controllables]] · [[prioritisation]] · [[effective-goal-setting]]

01:21:06 Speaker 9
My differences be a weakness. Stop causing me to be a victim in this journey and start making sure that they're my strengths. And I think that is a transformational attitude to take. And if you can genuinely adopt that, it will change everything about your journey. And the same is true for everybody because it did for me because being able to turn all the things that I that weren't right about me, that were different about me, that might be perceived as different about me in the way that you try and change them. I was feeling like I was a victim and just letting them rule my behaviour. And I appreciate they do a little bit still to this day. But if you knew me 20 years ago, you'd be shocked. Lash did and he'll know. But it was about recognising that actually I had a choice to use those things as a strength, but I had to control them.

01:22:03 Speaker 9
I had to target them, direct them. I had to be able to manage them in a way that they could be used as strength. People don't believe me when I say that I'm grateful for my childhood trauma. I genuinely am. I don't feel any anger or frustration towards my dad. In fact, I feel immense empathy towards him. And I feel really grateful for what I went through because I've moved on and I've stopped being a victim of that. And I've started to become somebody who recognises what that makes me into and then how to use that as a strength. But I need to be aware of it because otherwise the risk is that I can go down some pretty bad behavioural rabbit holes if I'm not careful.

01:23:01 Speaker 9
Even to the point where I've had to make some really, really strong decisions in my life about who I do and don't include in my life. Lauren will know this because we talk about it quite a lot. I live with my nan. To some extent, I was brought up by my nan. And at one point in my life, I basically stepped away from my nan and that entire side of my family because I realized that my cousin was my best man at my wedding and probably my best friend for a long part of my life. I walked away from those people because I realized they were having a toxic effect on me. A couple of those people really fucking hate me. But you know what? It was one of the best things I could have done because I didn't do it to make a point or be bitter or to other that. I did it just simply because it was the right thing for me to do. It was hard. It was hard to do, but sometimes you've got to make those decisions. Otherwise, what you do is you just end up continuing to.

01:23:59 Speaker 9
Create an environment that makes you a victim because you haven't got the strength to survive. I think that piece is really cool. Because what you are doing is you are starting to work out systems to stop yourself falling into that deho. You are starting to accept who you are, recognize the challenges it comes with, and hopefully take that next step, which is to actually realize how you can use that to your advantage. If you can control it rather than allowing it to drag you into that state of self sabotage all the time, and that's really cool.

01:24:39 Speaker 1
Bab and I think I don't know if Alexis was going to add something. He did have his hand up and he put his hand down. Were you going to add something?

01:24:49 Speaker 7
Yeah, it was something that Pete was talking about. We talked about the wants and the needs, and it's just something that I did recently. I think it was after one of our seminars. But I use a tool called Notion to take all my notes at work and personally. And I actually just asked it to create a prioritization matrix similar to the one we have with the delay, do, dump, and the diddles and all things like that. But I asked it to create categories based on work and social kind of structures that I have.

01:25:24 Speaker 7
So I've blended the two, so I don't just have one for work and one for social. I have one that kind of encapsulates both. I know what do I need to fit around my day or my week, not just treat them separately and hope that they combine well. If that makes sense, and that's actually helped quite a lot.

01:25:44 Speaker 1
I like that.

01:25:46 Speaker 9
I think that's brilliant, Alexis. And do you know what is brilliant about that? Don't get me wrong, I like the actual intricacies of the system that you've implemented there. It sounds great. The big thing there, the really big part there is the ownership piece. And you know, you've had a really cracking start to your time within the community. And, of course, when it's us, we don't know what we're doing differently from other people. And the reason that you are having such a good start is that ownership piece. What you're doing is Europe. You're and you talked about this. You wrote about this in your post, which I thought was really great. Which I think people should go and revisit if they can. I thought it was a fantastic post. What you've decided is you've decided to be proactive about owning some action that you're going to take off an idea because it's great. We talk about prioritization. We give people a model to use because you know what, you can find a better one that works for you. That's individualized, then use it. And it's about owning it. What you've done is you've decided right, I'm going to take ownership of.

01:26:41 Speaker 9
My ability to prioritize my journey is such that maybe I need something a little bit more intricate here or a little bit more individualized to myself. I'm going to make sure that prioritization is something that I do. I'm going to use a different process for doing it. That's fine. As long as you're prioritizing well and that making makes you that high functioning individual and that's absolutely fantastic. But what you've got to do is go away, be deliberate proactive and actually action it. The piece there is about at the very minimum, you're in that place of chosen ownership.

01:27:11 Speaker 9
Arguably purpose-driven ownership. And that's the difference. That level is the only level of ownership that you take over everything. The diff is largely the difference maker. It's not the same as level of commitment. It's a different thing. It's about who you are, and that's the key thing there, Alexis. The model is great. It's a lovely idea. Other people might ask you to show you it, but tell you. What if you're thinking about asking Alexis to show you? There's no point unless your level of ownership is at the right place.

01:27:41 Speaker 9
Because it will just become another nice thing that you have. I think that's the bit that stands out for me, Alexis. There is the level of ownership that you've demonstrated for that. And I also love the level of ownership that you have to supporting two nations, which will suit you nicely as the tournament goes on. Unless they meet in the final, then that's going to be interesting.

01:28:07 Speaker 7
Do. I suppose it's fair enough.

01:28:08 Speaker 9
And on the other hand, that's well, the problem for us is we just have to tolerate the one. On the other note, just going back to it, I won't take it completely off topic as well Pete behind you. Could you do me a favor and shut those drawers because I feel like I'm in one of the paranormal activity things. And my OCD is doing my head in. And those two drawers sticking out drive me around the bends for the last 90 minutes.

01:28:42 Speaker 8
Lauren, are you on mute? I don't know if you're on mute.

01:28:55 Speaker 9
Lauren's chatting away and I can hear her down the stairs, but you can't hear her.

01:29:11 Speaker 9
Lauren, I don't. I don't know. I can't hear you at all. I don't know if I could put your hands up if you can't hear Lauren. I can't hear her. None of us can hear you, Lauren. You're on mute at the moment. That's probably why.

01:29:26 Speaker 8
Now you're still on. It's called night talk.

01:29:35 Speaker 1
He's not going to do it now just to annoy you.

01:29:40 Speaker 1
I'm not on mute. Can you hear me? Yeah.

01:29:51 Speaker 8
I know. I don't know why I'm not on mute. Can you hear me? Yeah, I can hear you now. Definitely not on mute. Can you hear me?

01:30:05 Speaker 7
I think there's a massive delay.

01:30:10 Speaker 9
I just think there's a massive delay, Lauren. Do you want to come down here and come on my screen?

01:30:29 Speaker 1
Alright. I'm now. I think it took myself off mute, on mute, off mute. Can you hear me now?

01:30:39 Speaker 1
Can you hear me now? I am talking.

01:30:44 Speaker 3
It's going to be really freaky for you, Nick. Can you hear me? Can.

01:30:49 Speaker 1
Anybody hear me? Everyone's frozen. It seems I don't know. There's obviously a problem with my connection because everyone is frozen. Can you hear me? Can you hear me now?

01:31:11 Speaker 3
But we don't know if he's live.

01:31:13 Speaker 1
We can hear you. I'm just worried about if he's. It's really delayed so I don't know if you can or not. Sorry, there's obviously an issue with my connection because everyone's basically Butum. I think we better wrap it up there anyway. It's gone seven. I've no idea if you can hear me or not, because it's really delayed. For about five minutes if you can hear me or not. I think I'm back.

01:31:50 Speaker 3
We can hear you.

01:31:51 Speaker 1
Really come back just to wrap up. And just to say big thanks, everyone, for the questions that you've asked, because obviously that helps to give the seminar some great direction. So thank you for the questions. And then obviously your engagement on the session as well. So obviously we better wrap it up there. We seem to be having some tech issues this end anyway. So a big thank you for that. And we will, of course, resume next week because we didn't as expected get through quite as much as many of the points as initially planned. So we will resume and continue that next week. If anyone has got any specific questions they would like us to ask and discuss on the seminar, then please do make sure you send them in as well. So thanks so much everyone, go and enjoy your dinners.

01:32:36 Speaker 1
And for some of you, see you at swimming tomorrow at this week's Wednesday morning.

PART TWO: Coachability Concepts
Frameworks for growth and development

Chapter 5: Transactional State
Understanding Your Ego States in Communication
Introduction
Effective communication is the cornerstone of high functioning, not only in how we engage with others but also in how we relate to ourselves. When we communicate clearly and consciously, we create space for growth, learning, and meaningful connection.
This chapter explores the transformative power of Transactional Analysis, a psychological model developed by Eric Berne in the 1950s, which illuminates the different ‘ego states’ that influence every interaction we have. By understanding and mastering these states, we gain the ability to navigate life’s complexities with clarity, emotional intelligence, and presence - the hallmarks of high functioning.
Understanding which state you’re operating from - and which state others are in - is crucial for effective communication, receiving feedback, and personal development.
Understanding Transactional Analysis: The Three Ego States
At its core, Transactional Analysis invites us to recognise that we operate from one of three ego states at any given moment: Parent, Adult, or Child. These states shape how we communicate, make decisions, and respond to challenges.
Parent State
The Parent state contains the attitudes, feelings, and behaviours incorporated from external sources, primarily parents and authority figures. It is a repository of learned behaviours, rules, and beliefs absorbed during our upbringing. It manifests in two forms:
Controlling Parent: Critical, authoritative, rule-enforcing. Uses phrases like ‘You should…’, ‘You must…’, ‘That’s wrong.’ Can be judgmental and directive.
Nurturing Parent: Caring, supportive, protective. Uses phrases like ‘Let me help you…’, ‘Don’t worry…’, ‘I’ll take care of it.’ Can be enabling if overdone.
Adult State
The Adult state is characterised by objective, rational, here-and-now thinking. This is the optimal state for coaching and professional interaction. It represents rationality, objectivity, and presence. This state embodies learning, problem-solving, and balanced communication - the state where high functioning truly flourishes.
	•	Processes information objectively without emotional bias
	•	Makes decisions based on facts and current reality
	•	Asks questions to gather information
	•	Responds rather than reacts
	•	Neither judges (Parent) nor sulks (Child)
Child State
The Child state contains recordings of internal events - feelings and emotions - from early experiences. Rooted in emotions and spontaneity, the Child ego state can be free and creative, expressing joy and curiosity. Yet, it can also regress into maladaptive forms:
Needy Child: Seeking validation, dependent, emotional. ‘I need you to tell me I’m doing okay.’ Looks for external approval.
Manipulative Child: Using emotional tactics to get needs met. Tantrums, sulking, playing victim. ‘If you really cared about me, you would…’
Why This Matters for High Functioning
High functioning is not about relentless achievement or perfection; it’s about embodying presence, balance, and adaptability in every aspect of life. When we communicate from the Adult ego state, we are open, rational, and attuned - qualities that foster trust, learning, and genuine connection.
Conversely, defaulting to the Parent or Child states without awareness can lead to misunderstandings, conflict, and self-sabotage.
When the coachee operates from the Adult state, they are receptive to feedback, able to process information objectively, and capable of making rational decisions about their development. Regression to Child states can block learning and growth.
A person in Child state might hear feedback as criticism (triggering defensiveness) rather than useful information (which an Adult would process neutrally).
Passion Fit’s Adaptation: Centring the Coachee’s Journey
At Passion Fit, our focus is on empowering the coachee to remain primarily in their Adult state. After all, this is their journey, and our role as coaches is to support their growth by helping them maintain presence, clarity, and coachability.
That said, there are moments when it is appropriate, even necessary, to allow the Parent or Child states to emerge authentically. For instance, tuning into the Nurturing Parent within can help a coachee offer themselves compassion during difficult times. Similarly, embracing the free Child state can unlock creativity and vitality. The key lies in awareness: knowing when these states serve growth versus when they obstruct it.
Real-Life Examples
Navigating Stress in Training
Consider Sarah, a dedicated triathlete balancing work, family, and an ambitious training schedule. After a particularly exhausting week, she finds herself snapping at her coach during a session and feeling overwhelmed by self-doubt. Reflecting later, Sarah realises she was operating from a Controlling Parent state, harshly criticising herself for not meeting expectations.
Her coach gently encourages her to shift to her Adult state, inviting her to acknowledge the facts - she trained hard despite challenges - and to explore adjustments to her plan compassionately. By consciously moving into her Adult ego state, Sarah communicates with clarity and openness. She identifies practical solutions, such as tweaking her schedule and incorporating rest, rather than succumbing to emotional self-criticism.
This shift not only improves her relationship with her coach but also enhances her overall well-being and readiness to perform.
Managing Team Dynamics at Work
James leads a project team at a technology firm. During a critical meeting, a junior team member challenges a decision, triggering James to respond defensively. Later, he recognises that he slipped into a Controlling Parent state, reacting from a place of authority rather than curiosity.
He consciously chooses to return to his Adult state, fostering an inclusive dialogue that values diverse perspectives. By modelling Adult-to-Adult communication, James cultivates trust and engagement within his team. This approach reduces tension and encourages creative problem-solving, which ultimately drives the project forward with momentum and cohesion.
Developing Awareness and Mastery
Awareness is the foundation of mastery. The journey begins by observing your default ego states, especially under stress. Ask yourself: When do I tend to slip into Parent or Child? What triggers these shifts? When might staying in the Adult state be more effective?
The beauty of Transactional Analysis lies in its practical application. You can learn to pause, notice your internal state, and consciously choose your response. This practice transforms communication from reactive to intentional, elevating every interaction.
To cultivate this skill:
	•	Practice mindful self-observation throughout your day
	•	Reflect on recent conversations: Which ego states were present?
	•	Experiment with shifting into your Adult state during challenging moments
	•	Notice how your communication and relationships evolve
Over time, this conscious choice becomes second nature, strengthening your capacity for high functioning in all areas of life.
Key Takeaways
	•	Transactional Analysis identifies three ego states - Parent, Adult, and Child - that shape how we communicate and respond to situations.
	•	High-functioning communication primarily emerges from the Adult ego state, characterised by rationality, presence, and openness to learning.
	•	The Parent state can be nurturing or controlling, while the Child state ranges from free and creative to needy or manipulative.
	•	Awareness of your ego states in real time enables conscious choice, preventing reactive patterns that undermine growth.
	•	Passion Fit emphasises empowering coachees to maintain their Adult state while recognising when authentic Parent or Child states can support development.
Reflection Questions
	•	Which ego state do you default to under pressure?
	•	How does your transactional state affect your ability to receive coaching?
	•	When you receive critical feedback, do you respond (Adult) or react (Child)?
	•	Are there specific people or situations that trigger you into Parent or Child states?

Chapter 6: Direction/Support Matrix
Owning Your Level of Coachability
Introduction
In the journey to becoming high functioning (whether in our careers, personal lives, or athletic pursuits) one of the most transformative skills we can develop is coachability. Coachability is not simply about receiving advice; it’s about owning our growth, understanding our current needs, and actively engaging in the process of development.
The Direction/Support Matrix offers an insightful and practical framework to help us do just that. Originally inspired by the Situational Leadership model developed by Paul Hersey and Kenneth Blanchard in the 1960s, this matrix has been adapted here to focus squarely on the coachee’s role - the level of coachability we bring to our own development.
This framework recognises that different people need different things at different times in their development journey.
Understanding the Direction/Support Matrix
At its core, the Direction/Support Matrix uses two axes to map out where we are in our development:
Direction: How much guidance or instruction do we need? This axis reflects our competence and skill level. The greater our skills and experience, the less direction we require.
Support: How much emotional or motivational support do we need? This axis reflects our internal drive and emotional regulation. The more self-motivated and emotionally balanced we are, the less external support we need.
By plotting ourselves along these two dimensions, we gain clarity about our current coachability - and crucially, where we want to move.
The Four Quadrants of Coachability
High Direction + Low Support: TELLING
For those new to a task who need clear instruction. The coach provides specific guidance on what to do and how to do it. The coachee has low competence but may have high commitment.
High Direction + High Support: SELLING
For those developing competence who need guidance and encouragement. The coach explains decisions and provides opportunity for clarification. The coachee is gaining competence but may lack confidence.
Low Direction + High Support: PARTICIPATING
For those with competence but lacking confidence. The coach facilitates and supports efforts and shares responsibility for decision-making. The coachee has high competence but variable commitment.
Low Direction + Low Support: DELEGATING - THE OPTIMAL STATE
For autonomous, high-functioning individuals. The coach turns over responsibility for decisions and implementation. The coachee has high competence and high commitment. This is where we want to get to - self-sufficient and consistently motivated, requiring minimal instruction because competence is strong, and needing little external encouragement because internal motivation is steady.
The Negative/Positive Matrix
This framework also maps onto a Negative/Positive axis, helping identify whether the current combination of direction and support is serving the individual’s growth or hindering it.
Too much direction for a competent person feels like micromanagement. Too little direction for a novice feels like abandonment. The art is matching the approach to the person’s actual development level.
Real-Life Examples
Navigating the Matrix in Triathlon Training
Consider Sarah, a dedicated amateur triathlete. When she first started training for her first sprint triathlon, Sarah was in the high direction/low support quadrant. She was highly motivated (eager to push herself) but she lacked technical swimming skills and experience in race strategy. Her coach provided detailed instruction on swim technique, pacing, and nutrition, while Sarah’s inner drive kept her steadily engaged. This period was challenging, but her enthusiasm helped her absorb the direction she needed.
As Sarah grew more skilled, she transitioned toward the low direction/low support quadrant. She no longer needed constant technical guidance and felt confident managing her training schedule independently. Her motivation was consistent, and she was emotionally resilient - able to handle setbacks without losing focus. In this quadrant, Sarah found the high-functioning rhythm she aimed for, a place where her coachability was at its peak.
Coachability in the Workplace
James is a project manager at a tech company. He’s an expert in his field but has recently been experiencing burnout due to personal challenges. His competence remains high, but his motivation and emotional regulation have fluctuated. James finds himself in the low direction/high support quadrant - he knows what to do but struggles to maintain consistent energy and focus.
James’ manager recognises this and shifts their approach, offering more emotional support and encouragement rather than technical direction. They check in regularly to provide reassurance and help James manage his workload. Over time, James works through this phase and gradually moves back toward the low direction/low support quadrant, regaining his high-functioning groove.
Special Circumstances: Life’s Impact on Coachability
It’s important to recognise that where we sit on this matrix isn’t fixed. Life phases, transitions, or unexpected challenges can temporarily shift our needs for direction and support. For example:
	•	A new employee or athlete unfamiliar with the environment will naturally need more direction as they build competence.
	•	Personal hardships, such as bereavement or illness, may increase our need for emotional support even if our skills remain intact.
Acknowledging these shifts with compassion and planning for progression helps us avoid discouragement and maintain ownership over our development.
Ownership and Responsibility
The matrix is a tool, but the real power lies in how we use it. Taking ownership means being honest about our needs and actively seeking the right balance of direction and support. It means not waiting passively for others to guide us but engaging in self-coaching and regular self-assessment.
For those who coach themselves, this ownership is especially vital. Without external accountability, it’s easy to stagnate or become stuck in less productive quadrants. Regularly reflecting on your position, seeking feedback, and adjusting your approach based on this insight fuels continuous growth.
The path to high functioning is a dynamic process, one that requires both humility to ask for help and courage to stretch beyond comfort zones. Progress looks like moving steadily toward the low direction/low support quadrant, where competence and motivation meet in harmony.
Key Takeaways
	•	The Direction/Support Matrix is a powerful framework to understand and own your coachability by assessing how much direction and support you need.
	•	High-functioning individuals aim to occupy the low direction/low support quadrant, where competence and internal motivation are balanced.
	•	Life circumstances and new learning phases may temporarily shift your position on the matrix - this is normal and should be acknowledged with self-compassion.
	•	Taking ownership of your development means regularly self-assessing, being honest about your needs, and proactively seeking the right balance.
	•	Progress toward high functioning is dynamic; it requires ongoing reflection, feedback, and adjustment.
Reflection Questions
	•	What level of direction and support do you currently need?
	•	Are you moving toward greater autonomy or becoming more dependent?
	•	Do you ask for the type of coaching you actually need, or do you accept whatever is offered?
	•	How does your coachability change depending on the domain (work, sport, personal)?
